critique_points.point_001: The paper insufficiently engages with prior work on hallucinations in narrative and literary AI, lacking discussion and comparison with relevant studies in story generation, creative fiction summarization, or AI-assisted literary analysis.
critique_points.point_002: The experimental focus on a single novel excerpt from Patrick White’s The Solid Mandala is narrow and unjustified, with no discussion of generalizability to other authors, genres, or narrative styles.
critique_points.point_003: The evaluation lacks quantitative metrics and statistical analyses, providing no frequency counts, precision/recall, or standardized factuality assessments to objectively compare hallucination occurrences between models.
critique_points.point_004: No experiments explore fine-tuning or domain adaptation of the evaluated models on literary corpora, limiting insights into whether hallucination rates can be reduced through specialized training or prompt engineering.
critique_points.point_005: Prompt design is opaque, with no concrete examples or ablation studies analyzing the effect of prompt phrasing on hallucination rates, which hampers reproducibility and understanding of prompt influence.
critique_points.point_006: Attribution of Gemini’s improved hallucination reduction to vocabulary size alone is speculative, lacking controlled experiments or analyses isolating vocabulary effects from other architectural or training differences.
critique_points.point_007: The annotation process lacks transparency, with no reporting of annotator expertise, annotation guidelines, or inter-annotator agreement metrics, raising concerns about reliability and replicability.
critique_points.point_008: The paper does not discuss the downstream impact of hallucinations on practical literary applications such as critique, pedagogy, or automated analysis, limiting understanding of real-world significance.

summary: This paper investigates hallucinations in summarizing complex literary texts by large language models, specifically comparing ChatGPT (GPT-4o) and Google Gemini on excerpts from Patrick White’s The Solid Mandala. It identifies twelve distinct hallucination types that impair faithful representation of plot, character, temporal, and stylistic elements. While ChatGPT exhibits frequent errors, Gemini shows notable improvements, attributed in part to a larger vocabulary. The study highlights the challenges of coreference resolution, modality detection, and narrative comprehension for current LLMs, underscoring the need for enhanced linguistic and narrative modeling to reliably summarize literary fiction.
strengths: Provides a novel, detailed analysis of hallucinations specifically in literary text summarization, expanding focus beyond common domains.
Develops a comprehensive catalog of twelve distinct hallucination/error types grounded in linguistic and narrative theory, supported by rich qualitative examples.
weaknesses: Limited experimental scope confined to a single literary work without justification or discussion of generalizability to other texts or languages.
Lacks quantitative evaluation metrics, annotation protocol transparency, and controlled methodological analyses, which weakens reproducibility and objective assessment.
questions: How might the findings generalize to other literary genres, authors, or languages with different narrative conventions and complexities?
Could fine-tuning or prompt engineering techniques tailored to literary corpora significantly reduce hallucinations, and has this been explored?
title: How (Un)Fair is Text Summarization?
